title: The Spam Inbox
subtitle: P(A given B): conditioning on what you know
realWorldHook: Every spam filter and every 'customers who bought this also bought…' runs on conditioning — updating a probability once you know one more thing.
conceptSummary: Conditioning on B shrinks the world to just the B cases, then recounts how often A happens inside that smaller world.

scenario.role: You're tuning an email spam filter.

steps.title: 100 emails, 10 of them spam
steps.body:
Here's your inbox: 100 emails, and about **10** are spam.

A 'spam filter' isn't magic. It's just one question, asked well: *given a clue, how likely is spam now?*

That move — narrowing the world to the emails with a clue, then recounting — is [[conditioning|Updating a probability to account for something you now know. You ignore every case where the condition didn't hold, then recount within what's left.]].

steps.prompt:
Overall, ~10% of these emails are spam — that's the [[base rate|The overall frequency of something before you condition on any clue. Here, 10% of all email is spam.]].

Now imagine narrowing down to only the emails whose subject is **ALL CAPS** and screams **'FREE MONEY'**. Of *those*, what fraction do you think is spam?

options.label: Still about 10% — a clue doesn't change much
Maybe 30% or so — a bit higher

steps.revealMessage: Next you'll filter the inbox down to just the 'FREE MONEY, ALL CAPS' emails and recount the spam inside that slice.

revealByOption.a: It feels like one clue shouldn't matter, but imagine throwing away every email that isn't ALL CAPS and screaming 'FREE'. The slice that's left looks very different.
revealByOption.b: You're right that it climbs — but it climbs much further than 30%. Honest mail almost never shouts 'FREE MONEY' in all caps, so that slice is mostly spam.
revealByOption.c: That's the instinct to confirm — honest mail rarely screams 'FREE MONEY' in all caps, so the few that do are mostly spam. Let's count it.

steps.prompt: Tap the clue chips to condition the inbox. Watch the emails without the clue slide away, the survivors repack, and **P(spam) in the current view** leap as the world shrinks. Try 'FREE' + 'ALL CAPS' together.
Conditioning on 'FREE MONEY, ALL CAPS' shoved P(spam) from ~10% up to ~80%. Why did the spam percentage jump so far?

options.label: Filtering restricts the world to that slice and recounts — honest mail rarely shouts 'FREE', so spam dominates what's left
Adding the clue created new spam emails that weren't there before
The percentage didn't really change; it just looks bigger

feedback.correct: Right — conditioning shrinks the world. Once you keep only the 'FREE MONEY, ALL CAPS' emails, the honest ones are mostly gone and spam dominates what's left. Same emails, sharper question.
feedback.incorrect: Don't ask 'how much email is spam overall' — ask 'among emails with *this clue*, how much is spam.' When you condition on the clue, that slice becomes your whole world. Recount inside it.

byOption.b: No new emails appear — recall how the inbox just collapsed down to a smaller slice. Filtering only changes *which* emails you're allowed to count. Spam's share looks bigger because the honest mail dropped out of the slice.
byOption.c: It genuinely changes: 10/100 overall becomes ~8/10 inside the 'FREE MONEY, ALL CAPS' slice. The fraction is computed against a much smaller, spam-heavy denominator.

steps.hint: Among just the ALL-CAPS-'FREE' emails, how many are spam versus the total in that slice? Count spam over the new, smaller denominator.

steps.title: The filter is just conditioning
steps.body:
To condition on a clue B, make B your entire world and recount A inside it.

Don't ask 'how likely is spam overall' — ask 'among the emails with *this clue*, how often is spam?' The [[conditional probability|P(A given B): the chance of A once you restrict attention to the cases where B is true. It can be wildly different from P(A) alone.]] can be wildly different from the [[base rate|The overall frequency before conditioning — here, the ~10% of all email that is spam.]].

This one move — restrict the world, then recount — is the engine behind the most counterintuitive result in all of probability. That's next.
